items.title: Validate Candidates After Squaring
items.description: Whenever you square an equation involving radicals, you must check the resulting algebraic solutions against the original sign/domain constraints.
items.content: Squaring can introduce extraneous solutions because it removes sign information. After obtaining candidates from the squared equation, plug them back into the original (or at least verify the original left-hand side must match the nonnegative right-hand side). This prevents accepting solutions that only satisfy the squared form.
items.reasoning: The successful attempts consistently converted the equation into a simpler algebraic form by introducing an intermediate expression (a quadratic “lumped” term) and using substitution with a nonnegativity condition. They either (a) ensured that after squaring, only candidates satisfying the original sign/domain constraints were kept, or (b) structured the substitution so that squaring introduced no extraneous possibilities. The unsuccessful attempts typically made subtle mistakes in handling absolute values/case splits after completing the square (leading to incorrect retention of solutions) or in factoring/solving the post-squared equation but then failing to properly reject extraneous roots that violate the original equation’s nonnegativity requirements.

items.title: Use Substitution for the Radical’s Inner Expression
items.description: Reduce complexity by substituting the common quadratic expression under the square root into a single variable, then solve the resulting simpler equation.
items.content: When the equation’s radical depends on a repeated algebraic expression, substituting that expression (or a closely related shifted version) turns the problem into solving a lower-degree equation. After solving in the new variable, translate back carefully while preserving any domain constraints inherited from the square root.
items.reasoning: The successful attempts consistently converted the equation into a simpler algebraic form by introducing an intermediate expression (a quadratic “lumped” term) and using substitution with a nonnegativity condition. They either (a) ensured that after squaring, only candidates satisfying the original sign/domain constraints were kept, or (b) structured the substitution so that squaring introduced no extraneous possibilities. The unsuccessful attempts typically made subtle mistakes in handling absolute values/case splits after completing the square (leading to incorrect retention of solutions) or in factoring/solving the post-squared equation but then failing to properly reject extraneous roots that violate the original equation’s nonnegativity requirements.

items.title: Control Absolute Values by Direct Radical Reasoning
items.description: Avoid unnecessary case splits from \(\sqrt{y^2}=|y|\) by structuring the algebra so the radical stays as a square root of a nonnegative quantity.
items.content: If you introduce \(|\cdot|\) through identities like \(\sqrt{y^2}\), you must split on the sign of \(y\) and carry those conditions through to the final check. A more robust approach is to rewrite so that the square root remains \(\sqrt{\text{(nonnegative)}}\), letting the nonnegativity condition guide solution selection without branching errors.
items.reasoning: The successful attempts consistently converted the equation into a simpler algebraic form by introducing an intermediate expression (a quadratic “lumped” term) and using substitution with a nonnegativity condition. They either (a) ensured that after squaring, only candidates satisfying the original sign/domain constraints were kept, or (b) structured the substitution so that squaring introduced no extraneous possibilities. The unsuccessful attempts typically made subtle mistakes in handling absolute values/case splits after completing the square (leading to incorrect retention of solutions) or in factoring/solving the post-squared equation but then failing to properly reject extraneous roots that violate the original equation’s nonnegativity requirements.

items.title: Switch to an Explicit Coordinate Constraint Model
items.description: Represent the givens as direct equations in coordinates to remove ambiguity about geometry relationships.
items.content: When a diagram could be interpreted in multiple ways, place the key point (often the right-angle vertex) at the origin and align axes with the perpendicular segments. Then encode “lies on the circle” as equalities of squared distances to the center, turning the problem into solving a small system of equations.
items.reasoning: Successful attempts consistently chose an unambiguous coordinate model: they encoded the given right angle and segment lengths as perpendicular axes through the right-angle vertex, then used the circle-radius condition as two explicit distance-to-center equations. They resolved the resulting ambiguity by recognizing there are typically two symmetric center locations satisfying the same constraints, and they selected the physically/diagram-consistent one. Unsuccessful attempts instead relied on incorrect assumptions about which points lie on the circle, confused the requested distance with a known radius or chord distance, or used chord/power/angle reasoning without correctly establishing the geometry needed for those formulas; this led to contradictions or non-sense results.

items.title: Expect Two Symmetric Solutions and Resolve by Geometry
items.description: Solving the constraints often yields multiple valid centers; use diagram/feasibility to pick the correct one.
items.content: Algebra from symmetric circle conditions commonly produces two candidate locations for the center. Compute the target quantity for each candidate, then select the one consistent with the configuration (e.g., which side of the right-angle region the center must lie on) rather than trying to force a single solution algebraically.
items.reasoning: Successful attempts consistently chose an unambiguous coordinate model: they encoded the given right angle and segment lengths as perpendicular axes through the right-angle vertex, then used the circle-radius condition as two explicit distance-to-center equations. They resolved the resulting ambiguity by recognizing there are typically two symmetric center locations satisfying the same constraints, and they selected the physically/diagram-consistent one. Unsuccessful attempts instead relied on incorrect assumptions about which points lie on the circle, confused the requested distance with a known radius or chord distance, or used chord/power/angle reasoning without correctly establishing the geometry needed for those formulas; this led to contradictions or non-sense results.

items.title: Validate That Every Point Used in a Circle Formula Actually Lies on the Circle
items.description: Before applying chord/power-of-a-point/tangent facts, confirm the prerequisite incidences and perpendicularities.
items.content: Many incorrect attempts stemmed from applying chord-distance or power-of-point reasoning using incorrect assumptions (e.g., treating the wrong point as an endpoint on the circle or misidentifying the relevant chord). Always verify which labeled points are on the circle and which lines are chords vs. tangents, then apply the formula only when its geometric conditions are satisfied.
items.reasoning: Successful attempts consistently chose an unambiguous coordinate model: they encoded the given right angle and segment lengths as perpendicular axes through the right-angle vertex, then used the circle-radius condition as two explicit distance-to-center equations. Unsuccessful attempts instead relied on incorrect assumptions about which points lie on the circle, confused the requested distance with a known radius or chord distance, or used chord/power/angle reasoning without correctly establishing the geometry needed for those formulas; this led to contradictions or non-sense results.

items.title: Reduce Exponents Using Euler/Fermat with a Coprimality Check
items.description: Before simplifying huge powers, confirm the base is coprime to the modulus and then reduce exponents modulo the appropriate totient/order.
items.content: When working mod a composite number, first check gcd(base, modulus)=1; then use Euler’s theorem (or a known multiplicative order) to replace exponent n by n mod ϕ(modulus). This dramatically shrinks the exponent and makes repeated-squaring feasible while keeping the transformation valid.
items.reasoning: Successful attempts consistently (1) reduced the problem to modular arithmetic mod a fixed number, (2) used Euler’s theorem to reduce large exponents to a smaller remainder when the base is coprime to the modulus, and (3) computed only a small set of powers via repeated squaring, then combined results. The incorrect attempt failed because it introduced an exponent-decomposition plan that required careful bookkeeping of missing intermediate powers; it reused computed values inconsistently (effectively multiplying the wrong set/exponent factors), leading to a wrong final value. In contrast, the correct work either kept exponent reduction straightforward to a target (e.g., reducing to an exponent like “one less than the modulus’ totient remainder”) or computed the needed power chains in a disciplined way with verified multiplication.

items.title: Compute Powers by a Verified Repeated-Squaring Chain
items.description: Build the needed modular powers using an exponent-assembly plan that matches the factorization of the target exponent.
items.content: Successful solutions computed a small set of squares (e.g., powers at exponents 2,4,8,16,32) and then assembled the target exponent via multiplication corresponding exactly to its binary/explicit decomposition. This prevents arithmetic drift and ensures every multiplication corresponds to a legitimately computed factor.
items.reasoning: Successful attempts consistently (1) reduced the problem to modular arithmetic mod a fixed number, (2) used Euler’s theorem to reduce large exponents to a smaller remainder when the base is coprime to the modulus, and (3) computed only a small set of powers via repeated squaring, then combined results. items.title: Validate Exponent-Decomposition Arithmetic to Avoid Missing Factors
items.description: If you change the decomposition of the exponent (e.g., using n=81+2 or splitting into unusual sums), explicitly verify that every required intermediate power is accounted for with the correct exponent.
items.content: The incorrect attempt shows a common failure mode: reusing partially computed powers while guessing or mishandling the remaining exponent pieces can silently introduce wrong factors. To avoid this, after choosing a decomposition, list the exact powers you need and recompute any that aren’t directly available.

items.title: Count the Complement with a Correct Constraint Model
items.description: When an event is awkward to count directly, reformulate the probability using a complementary event whose constraints are easier to count.
items.content: For problems about “adjacent / not adjacent” selections on a circle, compute the number of valid selections with the adjacency restriction, then subtract from the total. This prevents missing cases and reduces complexity compared to counting configurations with at least one adjacency directly.
items.reasoning: The successful attempts share a clear structure: define the probability via a counting model, then switch to the complement event that’s easier to count (“no adjacency”), using a correct circular-constraint counting method. The incorrect attempt fails by miscounting the number of non-adjacent triples on a cycle: it sets up a gap-sum equation but omits key factors about how circular gap patterns correspond to distinct seat-sets (and/or uses an incorrect stars-and-bars transformation for the circular case). In general, the distinction is not algebraic but combinatorial modeling: correct handling of “cycle vs line” and ensuring the mapping from gap variables to actual subsets is bijective (or correctly weighted).

items.title: Use Circular “No-Adjacency” Counting Carefully
items.description: Selections on a cycle require a circular-specific counting method; importing a linear-gap argument can under/overcount.
items.content: If you use a gap (stars-and-bars) approach, ensure it properly accounts for the rotational/cyclic structure of the table and yields the correct circular count formula. A common failure mode is getting the right gap-sum equation but wrong correspondence/weighting between gap assignments and distinct seat subsets.
items.reasoning: The successful attempts share a clear structure: define the probability via a counting model, then switch to the complement event that’s easier to count (“no adjacency”), using a correct circular-constraint counting method. The incorrect attempt fails by miscounting the number of non-adjacent triples on a cycle: it sets up a gap-sum equation but omits key factors about how circular gap patterns correspond to distinct seat-sets (and/or uses an incorrect stars-and-bars transformation for the circular case). In general, the distinction is not algebraic but combinatorial modeling: correct handling of “cycle vs line” and ensuring the mapping from gap variables to actual subsets is bijective (or correctly weighted).

items.title: Validate the Mapping from Auxiliary Variables to Real Configurations
items.description: Whenever you introduce auxiliary “gap” variables, explicitly check that each assignment corresponds to exactly one valid configuration (or adjust for the exact multiplicity).
items.content: To avoid subtle counting errors, perform a bijection check in both directions: from any valid selection derive the auxiliary variables uniquely, and from any auxiliary assignment reconstruct the selection uniquely (or determine a constant multiplicity factor). This is especially important on cyclic structures where rotation can create duplicate representations.
items.reasoning: The successful attempts share a clear structure: define the probability via a counting model, then switch to the complement event that’s easier to count (“no adjacency”), using a correct circular-constraint counting method. The incorrect attempt fails by miscounting the number of non-adjacent triples on a cycle: it sets up a gap-sum equation but omits key factors about how circular gap patterns correspond to distinct seat-sets (and/or uses an incorrect stars-and-bars transformation for the circular case). In general, the distinction is not algebraic but combinatorial modeling: correct handling of “cycle vs line” and ensuring the mapping from gap variables to actual subsets is bijective (or correctly weighted).

items.title: Compute Prime Exponents with Legendre, Then Prove Positivity
items.description: For binomial coefficients, convert “is a prime divisor?” into checking whether the prime-adic valuation is strictly positive.
items.content: Model divisibility by writing the exponent of a prime in the factorial ratio and using a structured formula (e.g., Legendre’s floor-sum). Then decide divisibility by proving the valuation is > 0, not just by checking a few numerically “likely” primes.
items.reasoning: The successful approaches all use the same robust strategy: reduce the question “largest 2-digit prime divisor” to proving, via prime-adic exponents, when a candidate prime actually has positive multiplicity in the binomial coefficient. They then systematically narrow the search by identifying ranges of primes where higher prime powers cannot contribute, and where the floor-function expressions must cancel to zero. The main failure patterns in incorrect attempts were (1) premature bounding that ruled out the wrong prime range, or (2) confusing the conditions for “floor cancellation to zero” with the threshold for positivity, leading to stopping at an incorrect candidate (e.g., stopping too early at a prime that actually has exponent 0, or misplacing the critical cutoff between ranges where the exponent is positive vs zero). Another consistent issue was over-reliance on an imprecise “pattern” without justifying the range where it holds.

items.title: Justify When Higher Prime Powers Vanish
items.description: Before simplifying valuation expressions, rigorously check when terms from higher powers of the prime contribute or are guaranteed to be zero.
items.content: Many successes explicitly used inequalities like p^2 > upper bound to conclude only the first floor term matters, turning a complex sum into a manageable expression. Incorrect attempts often either assumed this without fully validating the range or applied the resulting simplification outside its valid interval.
items.reasoning: The successful approaches all use the same robust strategy: reduce the question “largest 2-digit prime divisor” to proving, via prime-adic exponents, when a candidate prime actually has positive multiplicity in the binomial coefficient. They then systematically narrow the search by identifying ranges of primes where higher prime powers cannot contribute, and where the floor-function expressions must cancel to zero. The main failure patterns in incorrect attempts were (1) premature bounding that ruled out the wrong prime range, or (2) confusing the conditions for “floor cancellation to zero” with the threshold for positivity, leading to stopping at an incorrect candidate (e.g., stopping too early at a prime that actually has exponent 0, or misplacing the critical cutoff between ranges where the exponent is positive vs zero). Another consistent issue was over-reliance on an imprecise “pattern” without justifying the range where it holds.

items.title: Find the Correct Prime Range via Floor-Cancellation Logic
items.description: When valuations involve floor functions, determine the exact interval of primes where floors share the same values and cancel to zero.
items.content: Successful solutions narrowed candidates by translating valuation conditions into inequalities about when ⌊200/p⌋ equals 2⌊100/p⌋ (yielding exponent 0) versus when it becomes large enough to leave a positive remainder. This turns a brute-force “check primes downwards” into a proof that no excluded primes can work.
items.reasoning: The successful approaches all use the same robust strategy: reduce the question “largest 2-digit prime divisor” to proving, via prime-adic exponents, when a candidate prime actually has positive multiplicity in the binomial coefficient. They then systematically narrow the search by identifying ranges of primes where higher prime powers cannot contribute, and where the floor-function expressions must cancel to zero. The main failure patterns in incorrect attempts were (1) premature bounding that ruled out the wrong prime range, or (2) confusing the conditions for “floor cancellation to zero” with the threshold for positivity, leading to stopping at an incorrect candidate (e.g., stopping too early at a prime that actually has exponent 0, or misplacing the critical cutoff between ranges where the exponent is positive vs zero). Another consistent issue was over-reliance on an imprecise “pattern” without justifying the range where it holds.

items.title: Use a Downward Search Only After Establishing an Upper Bound Cutoff
items.description: To find the largest valid prime factor, combine a theoretical cutoff that eliminates entire ranges with a final descending check near the cutoff.
items.content: Correct attempts confirmed that all primes above a certain threshold have valuation 0, then only tested the next few primes below that threshold to find the maximum. Failures often looked for patterns and then jumped to a candidate without a proof that every larger prime is impossible.

items.title: Validate Any “Pattern” with Interval Proof, Not Numerology
items.description: If you notice an observed cancellation pattern, prove the condition on which it depends across a whole interval of primes.
items.content: Some wrong solutions relied on an informal pattern about floors being 2 and 1 (or similar) without precisely locking down the interval where that pattern must hold. The fix is to derive the interval from inequalities and explicitly confirm boundary cases where the floors change.

items.title: Disjoint Case Split by Structural Role
items.description: When a condition depends on whether a special fixed element participates in the repetition, split cases on that participation to make counting disjoint.
items.content: First identify what drives the variation (e.g., whether the repeated digit equals the fixed leading digit). Then split into cases that cannot overlap and together cover all possibilities. This prevents double-counting and makes it easier to enforce “exactly one pair” in each subcase.
items.reasoning: The successful attempts share a consistent strategy: they explicitly model the digits as a multiset with “exactly one pair and two singles,” then split into disjoint cases based on whether the repeated digit equals the fixed leading digit or not. They carefully enforce “no other repetitions” by excluding digits that would create a second pair. The incorrect attempts typically fail in one of these ways: (1) case splits that are not actually exhaustive/disjoint, (2) an arithmetic slip due to incorrect subtraction of excluded digits, or (3) a logical misinterpretation of the condition—sometimes allowing a digit choice that would unintentionally create another repeated digit. The biggest transferable lesson is to formalize the constraint (“exactly one pair; others distinct”) and then validate, within each case, that the exclusions prevent additional pairs.

items.title: Encode “Exactly One Pair” as a Multiset Constraint
items.description: Translate “exactly two identical digits” into “one digit occurs twice and the other two digits occur once each,” then count accordingly.
items.content: Instead of informal reasoning, model the digits by occurrence counts: one repeated value with frequency 2, and two other distinct values with frequency 1. This clarifies what must be excluded and reduces the chance of accidentally allowing a second repeated value.
items.reasoning: The successful attempts share a consistent strategy: they explicitly model the digits as a multiset with “exactly one pair and two singles,” then split into disjoint cases based on whether the repeated digit equals the fixed leading digit or not. They carefully enforce “no other repetitions” by excluding digits that would create a second pair. The incorrect attempts typically fail in one of these ways: (1) case splits that are not actually exhaustive/disjoint, (2) an arithmetic slip due to incorrect subtraction of excluded digits, or (3) a logical misinterpretation of the condition—sometimes allowing a digit choice that would unintentionally create another repeated digit. The biggest transferable lesson is to formalize the constraint (“exactly one pair; others distinct”) and then validate, within each case, that the exclusions prevent additional pairs.

items.title: Enforce No-Extra-Repetition via Careful Exclusions
items.description: After counting choices for the repeated digit, explicitly exclude any values that would create additional pairs or triples.
items.content: Each time you pick digits, verify that the remaining slots cannot match either the repeated digit (to keep other digits single) or each other (to avoid a second pair). Many incorrect solutions come from choosing from sets that are missing or overbroad, effectively permitting an additional repetition.
items.reasoning: The successful attempts share a consistent strategy: they explicitly model the digits as a multiset with “exactly one pair and two singles,” then split into disjoint cases based on whether the repeated digit equals the fixed leading digit or not. They carefully enforce “no other repetitions” by excluding digits that would create a second pair. The incorrect attempts typically fail in one of these ways: (1) case splits that are not actually exhaustive/disjoint, (2) an arithmetic slip due to incorrect subtraction of excluded digits, or (3) a logical misinterpretation of the condition—sometimes allowing a digit choice that would unintentionally create another repeated digit. The biggest transferable lesson is to formalize the constraint (“exactly one pair; others distinct”) and then validate, within each case, that the exclusions prevent additional pairs.

items.title: Validate Counting Factors (Positions vs. Values)
items.description: Use a two-step check: first choose which positions receive the repeated digit, then count value choices for the remaining positions with correct exclusion counts.
items.content: Common success patterns separate “placement” (e.g., choose which slots contain the pair) from “labeling” (choose allowable digits for the singles). When results go wrong, it’s often because the exclusion count or the placement combinatorics was computed for the wrong scenario.
